1: Cornelius is a Roman centurion of the Cohors Italica — an auxiliary unit stationed in Caesarea Maritima (Herod's coastal capital of Judea). Inscriptions attest an Italica cohort in Syria-Palestine in the first century (CIL III 14137). He is described as eusebēs kai phoboumenos ton theon ('devout and God-fearing') — the latter a technical designation in Acts (Acts 10:2, 22, 35; Acts 13:16, 26) for Gentile sympathizers with Judaism who had not undergone full conversion (circumcision). God-fearers attended synagogues, gave alms to Jewish causes, observed some practices, but remained Gentiles. Cornelius's acceptance of the Spirit without prior circumcision becomes the precedent cited at the Jerusalem Council (Acts 15:7-11).
28: Jewish food laws and purity regulations created social barriers preventing shared table fellowship between observant Jews and Gentiles. The Mishnah (m. Avodah Zarah) regulated business and social contact with idolaters; meals in Gentile homes risked defilement through non-kosher food and vessels. Peter's vision of the sheet with unclean animals (Acts 10:11-16) — 'what God has made clean, do not call common' (ha ho theos ekatharisen sy me koinou) — addresses both food laws and their social function. Peter's insight (Acts 10:28) that he should not call any person common or unclean shows he understood the vision's primary referent was not food but people.
2: The category 'devout and God-fearing' (eusebēs kai phoboumenos ton theon) describes a well-attested class of Gentile synagogue-adherents in the first-century diaspora. Inscriptions from cities across Asia Minor and Aphrodisias specifically attest theosebeis ('God-fearers') as a distinct group separate from full proselytes: they attended synagogue, observed monotheism and basic Jewish ethics, and contributed to Jewish community welfare, but had not undergone circumcision. Josephus mentions God-fearing Gentiles in Antioch (J.W. 7.3.3). The 'Italian Regiment' to which Cornelius belongs identifies him as part of the Roman military aristocracy — a figure of substantial social prestige choosing Jewish religious affiliation.
3: The 'ninth hour' (three in the afternoon) was one of the three traditional Jewish prayer-times (Psalms 55:17 — 'evening, morning and noon I cry out'; m. Berakhot Acts 4:1). This corresponds to the time of the afternoon tamid (daily sacrifice) in the temple. Josephus describes devout Jews maintaining prayer at this hour even in Rome (Ant. 14.10.12). Cornelius's observance of the ninth-hour prayer while stationed in Caesarea — far from the temple — is the God-fearer's diaspora practice of Jewish liturgical rhythms without direct temple participation. The angel's appearance at the prayer-hour is Luke's consistent pattern: divine communications come at moments of deliberate devotion.
4: 'Your prayers and gifts to the poor have come up as a memorial offering before God' — the language of ascending sacrifice applied to prayer and almsgiving echoes Tobit Acts 12:12 ('I was sent to test you; at the same time, God sent me to heal you... When you prayed, I brought a reminder of your prayer before the Holy One'). The angel Raphael's similar function in Tobit — presenting prayers before God as a memorial — provides the conceptual framework: Cornelius's prayer and alms have been 'presented' to God as acceptable worship. Philo similarly describes prayer as equivalent to sacrifice in the life of the diaspora Jew (Spec. Leg. 1.272).
5: Joppa (modern Jaffa, south of Tel Aviv) was one of Judaea's few Mediterranean ports, approximately 55 kilometers south of Caesarea Maritima. Simon the tanner's location 'by the sea' (Acts 10:6) is both a geographic marker and a sociological detail: tanners required water for their craft and typically lived at the margins of settlements because of the smell. Peter lodging with a tanner situates him already on the social boundary — the guest of a ritually marginalized craftsman — before the vision pushes him further across the purity lines.
6: The tanner's profession (burseus — leather worker) placed him in a category of ritual marginality in rabbinic law: constant contact with animal carcasses made sustained Levitical purity extremely difficult. The Mishnah (m. Ketubot Acts 7:10) listed the tanner's trade as grounds for divorce if a wife objected to marrying into the profession; tanners were typically required to live 50 cubits from the edge of town (m. Baba Batra Acts 2:9). Peter's willingness to lodge with Simon the tanner thus already represents a relaxed attitude toward purity-boundary-maintaining — contextualizing the rooftop vision's more radical demand.
7: 'A devout soldier who was one of his attendants' (stratiōtēn eusebē tōn proskarterountōn autō) — Cornelius's household includes a soldier described with the same eusebēs (devout) as Cornelius himself. The Greek term proskarterountōn (those who attend/serve continuously) describes personal attendants or bodyguards. A centurion's household in Roman military culture included personal servants, freedmen, and soldiers assigned to his administrative service. The devout soldier represents the God-fearing influence extending through Cornelius's household structure.
8: Cornelius's sending of three men (two servants + one soldier) on a 55-kilometer journey to Joppa mirrors the precise number the Spirit reports to Peter ('three men are looking for you,' Acts 10:19). The two-way divine coordination — angel to Cornelius in Caesarea, Spirit to Peter in Joppa — is Luke's characteristic demonstration of divine orchestration operating simultaneously on two separated individuals. The journey of approximately 55 km on Roman roads would take about two days (consistent with 'the following day' in Acts 10:9 and 'the next day' in Acts 10:23).
9: Rooftop prayer was a recognized practice in Judaism; the flat roofs of Palestinian houses provided both privacy and the elevated orientation toward heaven associated with prayer. Rooftops appear throughout Jewish literature as spaces for prayer, meditation, and divine encounter: Daniel 6:10 (Daniel prays toward Jerusalem from his window); Judith Acts 8:5 (she pitched a tent on her roof for fasting); Tobit Acts 3:11 (Tobias went to the roof to pray). Peter's midday (sixth hour) prayer may represent a personal devotional practice beyond the three standard prayer times.
10: The 'trance' (ekstasis — literally 'standing outside oneself') is the technical Greek term for the visionary state in which ordinary consciousness is suspended and divine perception substituted. The LXX uses ekstasis for the deep sleep that fell on Adam during the creation of Eve (Genesis 2:21) and Abraham's vision (Genesis 15:12). Jewish apocalyptic literature uses the term for the prophetic state of reception (1 En. Acts 71:11; 4 Ezra 10:30-57). Luke uses it for Peter's vision here and Paul's temple vision (Acts 22:17). The combination of hunger-induced physical receptivity and the onset of ekstasis is a recognized pattern in ancient descriptions of visionary experience.
11: 'Heaven opened' (ouranon aneōgmenon) is the standard theophany-formula in Jewish and Christian visionary literature: Ezekiel's chariot-vision begins 'the heavens were opened' (Ezekiel 1:1 LXX); the Synoptics use it for Jesus's baptism (Mark 1:10); Revelation 19:11 uses it for the final vision. The 'sheet' (othonē — a linen cloth, sail-canvas) let down by four corners images both a container for the animal contents and a spatial metaphor for the four corners of the earth (Isaiah 11:12 — God gathering Israel 'from the four corners of the earth').
12: The three categories of animals (four-footed creatures, reptiles, and birds) follow the creation-taxonomy of Genesis 1:24-25 and Leviticus 11's clean/unclean divisions. The Levitical food laws (Lev 11; Deuteronomy 14:3-21) governed which animals could be eaten by Israel, functioning as a daily marker of covenant distinctiveness. The sheet contained panta ta tetrapoda (all the four-footed animals) — the 'all' (panta) is the key: both clean and unclean together, without distinction. The vision's content is not primarily about food but about the category-system of clean/unclean that the food laws instantiated.
13: The divine command 'Get up, Peter. Kill and eat' (anastas thuson kai phage) represents the direct overriding of Levitical food law by divine command. The imperative anasta (rise!) is the same command-form used throughout Luke-Acts for healings and resurrection-announcements. The combination thuson kai phage (kill and eat — using thyō, the sacrificial-slaughter verb) presents the meal as a quasi-sacrificial act — the food prepared for eating is to be treated as an offering, authorized directly by God rather than by Levitical regulation.
14: Peter's refusal — 'I have never eaten anything impure or unclean' (oudepote ephagon pan koinon kai akatharton) — uses the two key technical terms of Jewish dietary law: koinos (common, i.e., ritually non-sacred and therefore defiling) and akathartos (unclean). The rabbinic tradition preserved in m. Hullin discusses in detail which animal parts and preparation methods result in tum'ah (impurity). Peter's claim of lifelong dietary purity is plausible given his Galilean Jewish formation; it also establishes the magnitude of the vision's demand — not a minor adjustment but a complete reversal of lifelong practice.
15: 'Do not call anything impure that God has made clean' — the theological claim is that God's creative authority supersedes the Levitical categorization system. The same reasoning appears in Mark 7:19 ('Thus he declared all foods clean') as an editorial comment on Jesus's teaching, and in Paul's argument in Romans 14:14 ('Nothing is unclean in itself; but it is unclean for anyone who thinks it is unclean') and 1 Corinthians 10:25-26 (citing Psalms 24:1 — 'the earth is the Lord's and everything in it'). The common theological ground: God's ownership of creation overrides any secondary categorization derived from covenant-law regulations.
16: The triple repetition of the vision follows the biblical and rabbinic convention that a divine communication occurring three times establishes certainty and eliminates the possibility of misinterpretation. Joseph's dreams were interpreted as certain because of their repetition (Genesis 41:32 — 'the reason the dream was given to Pharaoh twice is that the matter has been firmly decided by God'). The three repetitions of Peter's vision follow this convention: the triple command removes any ambiguity about the divine will. The sheet's immediate withdrawal (anelēmphthē) before Peter has resolved the vision's interpretation is deliberate — he must hold the unresolved meaning in mind as the Cornelius messengers arrive.
17: The divine timing of the Cornelius messengers' arrival at the gate while Peter is still perplexed about the vision's meaning is Luke's characteristic demonstration of divine simultaneity: two events in two cities, coordinated by one divine director. The 'gate' (pylōna) of Simon the tanner's house is the entrance-passage of a courtyard house — the standard Palestinian domestic architecture where a gatehouse or gate-room (pylōn) provided access from the street to the inner courtyard. The Cornelius messengers standing at the gate while Peter is on the roof represents the physical distance between the purity-boundary (house interior) and the outside world.
18: The messengers' inquiry about 'Simon who is known as Peter' (Simōna ton epikaloumenon Petron) uses the double-name designation that Luke consistently employs throughout Acts for the apostle. The Aramaic Simon (the name Jesus gave; Matthew 16:17-18) and the Greek Peter (translation of Kepha/Cephas — rock) together signal the bilingual Jewish-Hellenistic context: Peter is identifiable by either name, reflecting his movement between Aramaic-speaking Palestinian Jewish contexts and Greek-speaking diaspora contexts.
19: The Spirit's direct address to Peter ('Simon, three men are looking for you') provides the interpretive bridge between the food-vision and the arriving Gentile delegation. The Spirit (pneuma) who speaks directly and audibly to Peter is the same Spirit who will fall on Cornelius's household (Acts 10:44-45), connecting the two events in a single pneumatological arc. The precision of 'three men' confirms that the Spirit has real-time knowledge of the situation at the gate — the divine orchestration operates with exact detail, not general providence.
20: 'Do not hesitate to go with them, for I have sent them' (mēden diakrinomenos hoti egō apestalka autous) — the Spirit's claim that Cornelius's Gentile messengers are the Spirit's own ambassadors is the theological watershed: the pagan centurion's servants are here declared to be divinely commissioned. Apostellō (to send, the verb from which 'apostle' derives) — the Spirit uses apostolic language for Cornelius's household servants. The Spirit's claim of ownership over the Gentile messengers will be the basis for Peter's defense in Acts 11:12 ('the Spirit told me to go with them').
21: Peter's hospitality to the Gentile messengers (Acts 10:23 — 'Peter invited the men into the house to be his guests') is itself a boundary-crossing act: Jewish purity rules discouraged hosting Gentiles in Jewish homes. The Mishnah (m. Oholot Acts 18:7) declares that 'the dwellings of Gentiles are unclean.' Peter's invitation of the three Gentiles into Simon the tanner's house — already a ritually marginal location — represents the first step of the practical boundary-crossing that the vision authorized.
22: 'Respected by all the Jewish nation' (martyroumenon hypo holou tou ethnous tōn Ioudaiōn) — Cornelius's reputation with the Jewish community in Caesarea is a significant social detail. A Roman centurion who was a God-fearer and philanthropist toward the Jewish community would have been known and respected across that community. Josephus mentions similar Gentile benefactors of Jewish communities in diaspora cities (J.W. 7.3.3). The messengers' description of Cornelius positions him as the most favorably situated possible Gentile recipient of the gospel — not a hostile Roman but a respected friend of Israel.
23: 'Some of the believers from Joppa went along' (Acts 10:23 — 'six brothers' according to Acts 11:12) — the witnesses traveling with Peter establish the Deuteronomic two-or-three-witnesses requirement for legally valid testimony (Deuteronomy 19:15). Their presence at Cornelius's house makes them the corroborating witnesses who will testify to the Jerusalem church in Acts 11:12 ('these six brothers also accompanied me'). Luke consistently documents the witness-structures that give apostolic events their evidential standing before the wider community.
24: Caesarea Maritima was Herod the Great's showpiece city, built between 22-10 BCE: a completely artificial deep-water harbor (the largest in the ancient Mediterranean), a Roman grid city plan, temples, an amphitheater, a hippodrome, and an aqueduct. As Rome's administrative capital of Judaea, Caesarea housed the Roman prefect/procurator's residence and the garrison of Roman troops. Cornelius's house in Caesarea — the Roman city par excellence — means that the first Gentile conversion takes place not in Jerusalem's Jewish heartland but in Rome's representative presence in Palestine.
25: Cornelius's falling at Peter's feet (pesōn epi tous podas proskunēsen) is the obeisance-gesture associated with divine or royal figures. Proskuneō encompasses the range from prostration before a god to bowing before a king to kneeling before a superior. In the Roman military context, soldiers performed structured gestures of respect before their superiors; Cornelius's prostration before Peter represents the highest-intensity respect gesture available, appropriate for someone he has received divine instruction to contact. The same gesture appears at Revelation 19:10 and Acts 22:8 (John falls before the angel) with the same correction: 'stand up.'
26: Peter's refusal of worship ('Stand up, I am only a man myself,' kai egō autos anthrōpos eimi) follows the consistent apostolic pattern of deflecting divine honors in Acts (Acts 14:14-15 — Paul and Barnabas tear their clothes at Lystra). The same refusal appears in the Greek-speaking world's descriptions of the proper response to divine attribution: Plutarch describes how virtuous rulers refused divine honors as a matter of philosophical propriety. The contrast with Herod Agrippa (Acts 12:21-23 — who accepts divine acclamation and is struck dead) will highlight the apostolic behavior as the correct posture before God-attributed glory.
27: The 'large gathering of people' (pollous synelēluthotas) that Cornelius had assembled represents the household-as-social-network: relatives (syngeneis) and close friends (ananagkaioi philoi — those bound by close obligation). The gathering of an extended household for a significant occasion was standard practice in Greco-Roman social life; the patron-client relationship structure meant that a centurion's household encompassed a broad social network. Cornelius's assembled household becomes the first Gentile community baptized as a group — the household as mission-unit rather than the individual as conversion-unit.
29: 'When I was sent for, I came without raising any objection' (anadikritōs — without discriminating/raising objections) — the word anadiakritōs echoes the Spirit's command not to 'discriminate/hesitate' (mēden diakrinomenos, Acts 10:20). Peter's report to Cornelius is the first step of the double-narrative structure Luke employs throughout the Cornelius episode: everything is reported twice (vision → report to Cornelius; Cornelius's vision → report to Peter; Peter's visit → report to Jerusalem). The double-narration serves Luke's historiographical purpose of providing multiple-witness confirmation for the theologically decisive event.
30: 'Three days ago I was in my house praying at three in the afternoon' — Cornelius's precise temporal reconstruction confirms the divine synchronization: the same ninth hour that was his regular prayer time (Acts 10:3) was the hour of the angelic visit. The 'man in shining clothes' (anēr en esthēti lampra) is described differently here (shining clothes) than in Acts 10:3 (where Luke simply narrates 'an angel'): the first-person report uses the phenomenological description (what Cornelius saw), while Luke's third-person narration uses the interpretive description (what it was). The same two-perspective technique appears in Acts 9 (Luke narrates Paul's Damascus encounter; Paul reports it in Acts 22:6-16; Acts 26:12-18).
31: 'God has heard your prayer and remembered your gifts to the poor' — the angel's message to Cornelius deploys two OT covenant-response formulas: God 'hearing' prayer (eisēkousthē sou hē proseuchē) echoes the Psalms' frequent affirmation of God's attentiveness to the righteous (Psalms 4:3; Acts 17:6; Acts 34:15-17); God 'remembering' (emnēsthēsan) is the covenant-memory vocabulary of Genesis 8:1 (God remembered Noah), Genesis 19:29 (God remembered Abraham), Genesis 30:22 (God remembered Rachel). The angel frames Cornelius's Gentile prayer and almsgiving within the covenant-response pattern that belongs to Israel.
32: The repeated mention of Simon the tanner 'who lives by the sea' (para thalassan) — first in the angel's message (Acts 10:6), now in Cornelius's report — is Luke's verification technique: the precision of the address (name, profession, location) is repeated exactly across the two narrative tellings, confirming the reliability of the communication. Joppa's location on the Mediterranean coast and the tanner's house 'by the sea' are geographically consistent: tanneries required constant water access.
33: 'We are all here in the presence of God to listen to everything the Lord has commanded you to tell us' — Cornelius's framing of the assembled household as a divine assembly (enōpion tou theou — before God) positions the Gentile gathering as a theophanic occasion. The language recalls the Sinai assembly ('Israel stood before God,' Exodus 19:17) and the synagogue service as a gathering before the divine presence. Cornelius's theological characterization of his own household gathering is more sophisticated than might be expected from a pagan soldier — the God-fearer's extended engagement with Jewish theological vocabulary is evident.
34: The phrase 'God does not show favoritism' (prosōpolēmptēs — one who accepts/shows the face/person) is a NT hapax (only here in the NT as a noun; the related verb prosōpolēmpteō appears in James 2:9). The compound represents the Hebrew nāśā' pānîm ('to lift the face/show favor'), a phrase used throughout the OT for showing partiality in judgment (Leviticus 19:15; Deuteronomy 1:17; Acts 16:19). Peter's theological insight — that what he had always believed about God's impartial justice in individual moral judgment now extends to ethnic inclusion in the covenant community — is the watershed theological realization of the chapter.
35: 'Everyone who fears him and does what is right is acceptable to him' — the principle of divine acceptance based on reverential fear and right-action rather than ethnic membership was not wholly new in Jewish tradition. The OT regularly affirmed that God's concern extended to all nations (Genesis 12:3; Amos 9:7 — 'Are not you Israelites the same to me as the Cushites?'). Philo argued for God's universal justice. The Noahide commandments (rabbinic tradition: b. Sanhedrin 56a-60a) defined the obligations incumbent on all humanity before God. Peter's insight builds on existing Jewish universalism but extends it to include Gentiles in the messianic community without requiring circumcision.
36: 'The message God sent to the people of Israel, announcing the good news of peace through Jesus Christ' — Peter's brief sermon rehearsal (Acts 10:36-43) for Cornelius's household follows the pattern of Paul's sermon in Acts 13:23-41 and the summary of early preaching preserved in 1 Corinthians 15:3-5. Both include: the John-Baptist context, the Galilean ministry, the Jerusalem death, the resurrection, the appearances, and the witness-claim. This consistent preaching structure (the kerygma) across multiple Acts speeches suggests Luke is working with a preserved summary of early Christian proclamation.
37: 'You know what has happened throughout the province of Judea, beginning in Galilee' — Cornelius's presumed knowledge of the Jesus-movement is plausible: as a centurion in Caesarea, he would have access to official reports, local gossip, and the accounts of Jewish informants about the religious teacher who had caused significant political disturbance in Jerusalem three to five years before. Josephus's reference to Jesus (Ant. 18.3.3 — the Testimonium Flavianum, even in its debated form) suggests that the movement was regionally known. Peter's appeal to what Cornelius 'knows' (hymeis oidate) grounds the proclamation in shared background knowledge.
38: 'God anointed Jesus of Nazareth with the Holy Spirit and power, and how he went around doing good and healing all who were under the power of the devil' — the summary of Jesus's ministry in terms of anointing (echrisen — from which Christos/Christ derives), Holy Spirit, power (dynamis), benefaction (euergetōn — the civic benefactor vocabulary), and healing against demonic opposition provides the theological framework in which the Jesus-story would be comprehensible to a Roman military audience: a divinely commissioned benefactor empowered by the divine spirit who liberated people from a hostile supernatural power.
39: 'We are witnesses of everything he did in the country of the Jews and in Jerusalem. They killed him by hanging him on a cross' (kremesantes epi xylou — hanging on a tree) — the apostolic witness-claim ('we are witnesses') is the foundation of the proclamation: the apostles are not transmitting doctrine but reporting events they personally observed. The crucifixion's public, state-sponsored nature meant it was verifiable by anyone who had been in Jerusalem during Passover — Cornelius as a Roman officer might have had access to Roman records of a public execution under Pontius Pilate.
40: 'God raised him from the dead on the third day and caused him to be seen' (edōken auton emphanē genesthai — allowed/caused him to become visible/manifest) — the third-day formula is among the earliest fixed elements of Christian proclamation (1 Corinthians 15:4 — 'raised on the third day according to the Scriptures'). The passive formulation ('caused him to be seen') emphasizes that the resurrection appearances were divinely controlled: God chose the witnesses, God selected the occasions. The passive voice consistently ascribes the resurrection to divine action rather than natural events.
41: 'Not to all the people, but to witnesses whom God had already chosen — by us who ate and drank with him after he rose from the dead' — the selectivity of the resurrection appearances (not to all Jerusalem but to specific pre-chosen witnesses) distinguishes the resurrection from hallucination or mass suggestion: the appearances were targeted and controlled by divine selection. The 'ate and drank with him' (sunepiomen kai synephagomen autō) formula is the bodily-resurrection apologetic: spiritual visions do not eat and drink; the resurrection appearances involved shared meals (Luke 24:41-43; John 21:9-13).
42: 'He commanded us to preach to the people and to testify that he is the one whom God appointed as judge of the living and the dead' — the title 'judge of the living and the dead' (kritēs zōntōn kai nekrōn) is the earliest attested formula for Jesus's judicial role at the final judgment (2 Timothy 4:1; 1 Peter 4:5). In the Roman legal context (Cornelius's world), the appointment of a specific judicial authority (horistheis hypo tou theou — appointed/designated by God) as the definitive court of appeal would be comprehensible. The resurrection (Acts 10:40) is the basis for this judicial appointment: the one who conquered death has authority to preside over all life and death.
43: 'All the prophets testify about him that everyone who believes in him receives forgiveness of sins through his name' — Peter's appeal to 'all the prophets' (pantes hoi prophētai) corresponds to the 'Moses and all the Prophets' of the Emmaus road exposition (Luke 24:27) — the claim that the entire prophetic corpus witnesses to Jesus. Specific prophets cited in Acts include: Joel (Acts 2:17-21), Amos (Acts 15:16-17), Isaiah (Acts 7:17-52; Acts 8:32-33; Acts 28:25-27), and the Psalms (Acts 2:25-28; Acts 4:25-26). The universal-scope 'everyone who believes' (panta ton pisteuonta) will be immediately tested and demonstrated by the Spirit's falling on the uncircumcised Gentile hearers before Peter finishes speaking.
44: The Spirit's interruption of Peter's sermon before its conclusion — 'while Peter was still speaking these words' (eti lalountos tou Petrou ta rhēmata tauta) — is both dramatic and theologically decisive: the Spirit does not wait for formal completion of the gospel presentation, the invitation, the prayer, or the response. The Spirit falls on faith-generated receptivity, not on a completed ritual sequence. This 'anti-ritual' aspect of the Cornelius Pentecost will be Peter's strongest argument to the Jerusalem church (Acts 11:15 — 'as I began to speak, the Holy Spirit came on them as he had come on us at the beginning').
45: 'The circumcised believers who had come with Peter were astonished' (exestēsan — were beside themselves, thoroughly amazed) — the word existēmi (to stand outside oneself) is related to ekstasis (trance, Acts 10:10) — the same family of words for being outside ordinary consciousness. The Jewish Christian witnesses to Cornelius's Pentecost are as 'beside themselves' with astonishment as Peter was in his trance — the divine surprise operates equally on both recipient and observer. 'Even on Gentiles' (kai epi ta ethnē) — the kai (even, also) is the shock-marker: the Pentecost gift has crossed the boundary that Jewish theology had maintained between Israel and the nations.
46: 'They heard them speaking in tongues and praising God' — the twin signs of Gentile Spirit-reception (glossolalia + praise) match exactly the Acts 2 Pentecost signs (Acts 2:4 — spoke in tongues; Acts 2:11 — declaring the wonders of God in our own languages). Luke's consistent deployment of the Pentecost sign-pattern at each geographic expansion of the Spirit's reach (Jerusalem, Samaria/Acts 8, Caesarea, Ephesus/Acts 19) creates a narrative theology of Spirit-geography: the same Spirit who inaugurated the Jewish-Christian community at Pentecost is progressively claiming new territory without modification or diminution.
47: 'Surely no one can stand in the way of their being baptised with water?' (mēti to hydōr kōlysai dynatai tis) — the baptism-prevention question (kōlyō — to prevent, hinder, forbid) appears again at Acts 8:36 ('what prevents me from being baptized?') and in early Christian baptismal liturgy (Didache 7; Justin Martyr, Apology 1.61). The rhetorical question form — 'can anyone prevent?' — is the standard formula for baptismal inclusion: the burden of proof rests on preventing, not on baptizing. The Spirit's prior gift makes water-baptism the formal seal of what has already occurred inwardly.
48: 'Baptised in the name of Jesus Christ' (en tō onomati Iēsou Christou) — the single-name baptismal formula in Acts (Acts 2:38; Acts 8:16; Acts 10:48; Acts 19:5) has been extensively debated relative to Matthew 28:19's trinitarian formula. The two formulas are not contradictory: baptism 'in Jesus's name' identifies the authority and community into which one is baptized; the trinitarian formula specifies the divine persons who are invoked. Early Christian practice likely varied, with Acts reflecting the primitive Aramaic formula and Matthew the developed Greek liturgical form. 'They asked Peter to stay with them for a few days' — the post-baptismal table-fellowship (implied in the extended stay) sealed the social integration that the Spirit and water had enacted.
